summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software developers make terrible user experience designers because they are fundamentally different from typical users. Drawing on Alan Cooper's concept of 'Homo Logicus,' he explains that programmers are outliers who thrive on complexity and control, while normal users prioritize simplicity and success. The post uses the Windows 95 'Find File' function as an example of how features that seem valuable to developers actually burden regular users. Atwood concludes that building simple, usable software requires developers to consciously abandon their natural instincts and think like ordinary humans.
key_points: Developers are 'freakishly outside the norm' and represent edge conditions, not typical users
Homo Logicus trades simplicity for control and accepts failure in exchange for understanding how things work
Normal users see additional features and parameters as more opportunities to make mistakes, not as valuable flexibility
The engineer joke perfectly illustrates the programmer mindset: prioritizing understanding the problem over surviving it
Building simple applications requires more skill than building complex ones nobody can use
money_quotes: Of all the professional hubris I've observed in software developers, perhaps the greatest sin of all is that we consider ourselves typical users.
We're not even remotely average – we are the edge conditions.
If you are letting me design your software, your project is in trouble.
The price of control is always more effort and increased complexity.
Homo logicus are driven by an irresistible desire to understand how things work. By contrast, Homo sapiens have a strong desire for success.
While programmers also want to succeed, they will frequently accept failure as the price to pay for understanding.
Anybody can build a complex application that nobody can figure out how to use. That's easy. Building an application that's simple to use... well, now that takes actual skill.
key_insight: Developers must recognize that their love of complexity and control makes them the worst possible judges of what normal users need, and great software design requires consciously overriding their natural instincts.
